Recently, CRWV announced new agreements with Meta (META) - in a deal valued at about $21B -and Anthropic (ANTHRO), as well as $6B deal with the trading firm of Jane Street.

Investors are punishing capital-intensive companies like CoreWeave, which expects CapEx of between $30B–$35B in FY26, double compared to FY25. The increasing CapEx is a result of massive demand for AI infrastructure, which is reflected in 850MW of active power in FY25 - adding 260 MW in Q4 across 43 active data centers, up from 32 in the previous year end- and the target of 8GW by 2030, which is more than 8x capacity growth.

A deep dive into the company’s guidance shows revenues of $12–$13B, representing 140% YoY growth, with long-term ARR above $30B. If I annualize Q4’25 revenue of $1.6B, current ARR is close to $6–$7B, which means that within two years ARR will grow about 5x.

Just impressive!

Also, CWRV’s long-term target of an operating margin of 25%–30% seems ambitious based on the current unprofitability. However, investors should know that this target will come not only from bare-metal services but also from its software solutions.

Currently CRWV is Mostly a Bare-Metal Provider, Long-Term Could Also Be a Significant AI Platform Provider

Let me break down my thoughts into three groups: advanced bare-metal provider, AI inference, and software/platform solutions.

Advanced bare-metal provider

It is well known that CRWV is reporting impressive revenue growth over the last quarters. The reason behind the company achieving triple-digit revenue growth is its advanced bare-metal performance. CRWV offers bare-metal solutions, which means that the training of AI models runs straightforward on CPUs, GPUs, etc., while vGPU setups train models via virtual machines.

Experts noticed that “bare metal can deliver up to 30% higher performance compared to vGPU setups for training large transformer models”. CRWV support that it can offer 20% higher GPU performance compared to other solutions, given that it achieves Model FLOPS Utilization (MFU) exceeding 50% -MFU is a metric about the actual performance of hardware during the training process.

Given that about $5B of its revenue is estimated to be tied to model training contracts, this answers the question of why CRWV’s AI infrastructure solution sees strong demand, to the point that even prices of NVIDIA’s (NVDA) older GPUs, such as A100, which CRWV uses, significantly increased in FY25, reflecting how efficient the company’s operating model is.

Inference Solutions

CRWV offers bare-metal solutions for the training and deployment (inference) of LLM models. As already mentioned, training is the main purpose of its solutions. However, inference could be the company’s most misunderstood component. First, trained LLM models, at some point, will need AI infrastructure in order to run, and CRWV has already achieved wins, given that Meta leverage its AI cloud platform to scale inference workloads, following their initial deal for training purposes.

A second win is the already mentioned deal with ANTRO, which “is expected to begin coming online later in 2026, and that Anthropic will use CoreWeave’s cloud platform for production (inference) workloads”.

A third win is the agreement with Perplexity AI, in order for CRWV to support inference workloads. I believe that CoreWeave could achieve more inference deals in the following years, in addition to its existing training contracts, because of its advanced technology due to bare-metal solutions, and in my opinion, this aspect is misunderstood by the market.

Now, take all the above and combine it with experts’ estimates of: "(CRWV’s) 2.5-year cash payback period on infrastructure investments, including prepayments”, as well as the fact that the company’s average weighted contract length increased to 5 years from 4 years.

Even more, take into account, that according to economist the adoption and productivity of AI are at an early stage. Most companies spend massive amounts to train their LLM models, but soon they will need to inference these models -as the above-mentioned inference wins suggest - and CRWV is well positioned to capture this market. Most importantly, it needs only 2.5 years to gain back its initial investments, while the inference period will take place over a longer time.

Even more, take into account, that according to economists, the adoption and productivity of AI are at an early stage. Most companies spend massive amounts to train their LLM models, but soon they will need to inference these models - as the above-mentioned inference wins suggest - and CRWV is well positioned to catch this market. Most importantly, it needs only 2.5 years to gain back its initial investments, while the inference period will take place for a longer time.

Software/Platform Solutions

CoreWeave is expanding its business lines. Already, 80% of large customers use its storage solutions, while CRWV’s cluster management platform, called Slurm on Kubernetes (SUNK), is already licensed by other companies. Also, CRWV offers W&B Models - which is like a dashboard and control system during the training of AI models - and Mission Control - which is like the control room of a data center - software that will be tested by NVDA.

Additionally, the acquisition of Marimo enables developers to create their own custom AI apps, while Monolith’s acquisition will expand CRWV’s AI solutions to the industrial market -AI in the real physical word.

In my view, CRWV expands horizontally with new customers, and vertically as it offers not only hardware, but also software/platform solutions. I foresee new inference deals in the following years, while software solutions could be a major catalyst for the company’s target of a long-term margin between 25%–30%.

Valuation and Conclusion

Revenue is projected to grow 142% in FY26, while by 2030 analysts expect more than solid double-digit growth. I predict that the company could achieve even higher revenue growth, given that software solutions are not embedded in its guidance.

The P/S ratio is 12.15, down 19% from its 12-month average of 15.66, reflecting an undervalued company.

Considering all the above, I see that the company has achieved some degree of de-risking in terms of customer concentration and debt financing, but it is still sensitive. Aside from its high debt, the company remains unprofitable with negative FCF. This means that it has no room for error or execution delays. If any of these happen, investors will easily push the stock price back to the $80–85 level, if not even lower.

Long term, I believe that the company can outperform, and the market is mispricing the stock, as it overlooks the upcoming inference wave, its advanced bare-metal tech, and the promising growth catalysts.

Overall, I anticipate the company will beat its guidance for full-year end 2026 and I rate the stock as a Strong Buy. Notably, due to the stock being high-risk, high-reward, I suggest investors approach it with a DCA strategy.

data.title: INTL: A Global Ex-US Alternative
data.description:
Main International ETF Outlook

International equities had very favorable performance in 2025 and have continued to hold their ground amid selling pressures in 2026. The Vanguard Total World Stock Index Fund ETF has outperformed the S&P 500 by around 9% percentage points since the beginning of 2025.

The Main International ETF (INTL) is one intriguing vehicle to consider, as its active global macro strategy has led to significant outperformance of other international ETFs. INTL targets a variety of international ETFs, providing diversification from the S&P 500 and the potential to outperform other global indices like the MSCI World Index.

The Main International ETF invests in reasonably priced global equities, including both developed and emerging markets, and its more active global macro approach should support continued outperformance.

The main downside of INTL is that it charges a relatively higher management fee and invests in ETFs that retail investors can access on their own.

Main International ETF Overview

The Main International ETF is a relatively newly listed ETF that invests in a basket of international ETFs. It charges a 0.89% management fee and has a 0.84% net expense ratio. INTL manages around $214 million in assets and has relatively lower liquidity, as its average trading volume is only around 16,000 shares/day.

INTL has had very favorable performance since it was listed in 2022, performing roughly in line with other indices like the MSCI ACWI ex. USA Index.

The Main International ETF invests in the following ETFs, providing it with a blend of exposure to developed markets and emerging markets in markets outside of the United States. The top 5 ETFs it invests in account for around 88% of its assets.

INTL uses an active strategy and occasionally changes its ETF composition. For example, it previously held a Mexico ETF as of July 2025. Its current composition focuses on a blend of equities in Europe, Japan, Latin America, Pacific markets, and emerging markets.

According to Morningstar, INTL primarily targets financial services, industrials, and technology companies, with no significant deviation ( 3% or lower) from its benchmark index for each sector. This setup also provides lower exposure to themes like consumers and still has strong tech exposure, which could make it more vulnerable to pullbacks. Moreover, INTL currently has lower exposure to energy stocks, although it is still slightly overweight energy. Overall, this sector setup is not very unique relative to peer ex.US international ETFs.

While INTL has outperformed other global ETFs with exposure to the United States, it has still slightly underperformed other global ETFs like the iShares Core MSCI Total International Stock ETF (IXUS), which does not have exposure to the US equity markets. IXUS invests directly in global equities, which is one reason it has slightly outperformed INTL through its lower fee structure. ETFs like IXUS should continue to slightly outperform INTL in the long run.

2025 displayed the appeals of a global investing strategy, as the combination of valuation expansions and positive currency performance translated into market outperformance in 2025, while the S&P 500 lagged behind. Moreover, many global indices are still heavily focused on the United States and Japan, making INTL's diversification efforts more unique.

MSCI World Index

The Main International ETF looks like a strong global diversification vehicle, and a great place to start for those who want to diversify away from the S&P 500 and other US-dominated indices.

Overview of Some of the Top ETFs Targeted

INTL is relatively easy to analyze since the majority of its assets include 5 ETFs. Many of these markets are attractively priced at the moment relative to the S&P 500.

Index PE PB Dividend Yield
MSCI Europe Index 16.76x 2.32x 3.0%
MSCI Japan Index 18.61x 1.77x 2.0%
MSCI Emerging Markets Index 16.52x 2.16x 2.3%
MSCI Pacific Index 18.73x 1.84x 2.5%
MSCI EM Latin America Index 12.33x 2.17x 4.7%

The top 3 ETFs it targets collectively account for nearly 70% of its assets, and include a blend of equities in Europe, Japan, Latin America, and other emerging markets.

1) The iShares Core MSCI International Developed Markets ETF (IDEV) targets leading developed markets like Japan and other European markets. IDEV has returned by around 8-9% per annum since its inception, and looks like a stable dividend play as it yields around 3.3% at the moment.

2) INTL invests 17.6% of its assets in the iShares Core MSCI Emerging Markets ETF (IEMG). IEMG trades at over 17x earnings due to the rally of dominant Asian markets like Taiwan, India, and South Korea. Based on the latest data from INTL, the iShares Core MSCI Emerging Markets ETF accounts for around 18% of its assets. It has limited exposure to other Latin American and MENA markets, although INTL also invests in a Latin America-focused ETF to broaden its emerging market exposure.

3) INTL invests around 12.3% of its assets in the State Street Europe Portfolio ETF (SPEU), which primarily targets financials, industrials, and healthcare stocks (around 57% of its total assets). SPEU has returned around 8-9% per annum over the past decade, and trades in line with the MSCI Europe Index in terms of its yield and valuation.

INTL also offers smaller exposure to Latin American and Pacific markets, providing more diversification in its portfolio.

4 INTL also invests in the iShares Latin America 40 ETF (ILF), which has had strong performance in recent quarters. Morgan Stanley recently noted that Latin American equities are currently traded at multi-decade low valuations. 2025 was a record year for this market, which has consistently returned around 9% per annum in the past decade.

5 INTL also invests around 8% of its assets in the Vanguard FTSE Pacific ETF (VPL), which targets key markets like Japan, South Korea, Australia, Hong Kong, and Singapore. However, VPL also heavily focuses on Japan, much more so than other global ETFs.

Overall, these ETFs have had favorable performance since late 2017, especially since Latin American equities have rebounded strongly to catch up with other global equity markets.

Risks and Alternatives

If you are targeting global ETFs, it is best to avoid ones with large exposure to the United States and to see if the ETFs employ active strategies to aim for outperformance. INTL fits the bill in this sense and has proven its outperformance potential since its listing in 2022.

Below are some key risks and limitations to consider:

  • INTL has much lower liquidity relative to other global ETFs
  • Global growth projections have declined following tensions in the Middle East, which increases the risk of markets like Japan and the EU potentially entering a recession. Emerging markets may also begin to struggle with a new wave of inflation, following their post 2022 recovery.
  • INTL's management fee is relatively high, especially since it only invests in ETFs, resulting in a second layer of fees.
  • All of its constituent ETFs are very easy for US retail investors to invest in, making it easy for investors to build similar exposure on their own to avoid this management fee
  • Valuations of many global markets, except for Latin America, have risen to new highs following their 2025 rallies. While they trade at a strong discount to the S&P 500, some markets like Europe do not trade at a strong historical discount and are not that cheap when compared to similar industries in the United States.

I have INTL ranked as a hold because I think it will continue to outperform other global ETFs with exposure to the S&P 500, and I think it is ideal to diversify away from the S&P 500. However, other international ETFs have a similar country and sector approach and have a lower fee structure.It is also very easy to mimic this investment style and avoid the extra fees by choosing country or regional ETFs. This opportunity would also allow investors to adopt a more overweight approach to cheaper markets like Latin America.

2026 will likely be a volatile year for global equities, but these regions appear reasonably valued at the moment and look like safe holds.
